resume:
Dana Okafor
Senior Data Engineer / Analytics Engineering
Toronto, ON (Remote-first)

SUMMARY
Senior data engineer with seven years building and operating the data platforms that analytics, ML, and product teams depend on. My center of gravity is the warehouse and the transformation layer: I build the pipelines that land raw events and operational data, model them into trustworthy datasets, and keep them fast, fresh, and correct. I have lived through the migration from hand-written cron-and-bash ETL to a modern, tested, version-controlled transformation stack, and I have the scar tissue to prove it. I treat data like a product: it has consumers, SLAs, contracts, and an on-call rotation, and I get genuinely uncomfortable when a number on an executive dashboard cannot be traced back to a tested model and a known source.

EXPERIENCE

Meridian Health Tech (digital health platform) — Senior Data Engineer — 2021 to present
I am on the central Data Platform team, four engineers, supporting roughly forty analysts and data scientists across product, clinical operations, and finance. I own the transformation layer and the warehouse cost and reliability posture.
- Led the re-platforming of our analytics stack onto a cloud warehouse with a version-controlled transformation framework. The old world was a tangle of scheduled SQL scripts with no tests and no lineage; a broken upstream column could silently corrupt a finance dashboard for days before anyone noticed. I introduced a layered modeling approach (staging, intermediate, marts), added data tests on the critical models, and wired lineage so a schema change surfaces its blast radius before it ships.
- Built the event-ingestion pipeline that lands product clickstream into the warehouse, including a schema-contract step that quarantines malformed events instead of letting them poison downstream tables. This cut the recurring 'the numbers look wrong' fire drills dramatically, though I will be honest that getting product engineers to respect the contract was more of a political project than a technical one.
- Owned a warehouse cost-reduction effort after our compute spend spiked. I profiled the most expensive recurring queries, restructured the heaviest models to be incremental rather than full-refresh, and introduced clustering on the hottest tables. Spend came down meaningfully and, more importantly, stopped growing faster than usage.
- Set up the data on-call rotation and freshness SLAs for the tier-one datasets, including alerting that pages when a critical model is stale beyond its contract.

Quillstream Media — Data Engineer — 2017 to 2021
Worked on the data team for an ad-supported streaming product.
- Built and maintained batch pipelines aggregating playback and ad-impression events for reporting.
- Migrated a set of fragile Airflow DAGs to a more modular structure and added retry and backfill discipline.
- Partnered with analysts to define the canonical 'active user' and 'watch session' definitions, which had previously meant three different numbers in three different reports. Getting agreement on a single definition was less a SQL problem than a negotiation between teams who each had a reason to prefer their own number; I ran the working sessions, wrote the definition down, and encoded it once in a model everyone had to read from.
- Ran the on-call for the batch pipelines for two years, which taught me more about reliability than any design doc. Most of our 3am pages were not exotic; they were a late upstream file or a silent schema change, and that pattern is exactly what pushed me toward contracts and freshness alerting in my next role.

NOTES FROM THE FIELD
A few things I believe after seven years, because they shape how I work. First, the most expensive data problems are almost never the query; they are the lack of lineage, because without lineage every incident becomes an archaeology project and every schema change is a gamble. Second, cost and correctness are usually the same conversation: a model that full-refreshes the world every hour is both expensive and fragile, and making it incremental often improves both at once, provided you handle late-arriving data carefully. Third, the hardest part of a data contract is social, not technical; the quarantine step that rejects malformed events is easy to write and hard to get an upstream team to accept, and I have learned to bring them the cost of the fire drills rather than a lecture about schemas.

SKILLS
SQL (expert), Python, cloud data warehouses, version-controlled transformation frameworks, data testing and data-quality contracts, orchestration and scheduling, dimensional and layered data modeling, incremental model design, warehouse cost optimization, lineage and observability tooling. I am pragmatic about tools; I care that the data is correct, fresh, and affordable, not which logo is on the pipeline. I am comfortable reading a query profiler and an execution plan, and equally comfortable explaining to a finance stakeholder why their number changed and whether the old one or the new one was right.

WHAT MENTORING LOOKS LIKE FOR ME
A large part of a senior role is making the analysts around me better, not just shipping my own models. In practice that means reviewing their transformation pull requests for things like fan-out joins and untested grain changes, pairing with them when a model is slow, and writing down the patterns so the next person does not relearn them painfully. I would rather spend an hour teaching an analyst why their join doubled their revenue number than silently fix it myself, because the second approach guarantees I will fix it again next month.

EDUCATION
B.Sc. Computer Science.

WHAT I'M LOOKING FOR
A senior data engineering role where the data platform is treated as core infrastructure with real consumers and real SLAs, not a side project. I want to work somewhere that cares about correctness and lineage, where cost is managed deliberately rather than panicked about once a quarter, and where I can keep mentoring analysts to write trustworthy models rather than just firefighting broken dashboards. I am most useful in an environment that already feels the pain of bad data reaching a customer, because that is when the work I care about is treated as essential rather than optional.
jd: Northwind Analytics is hiring a Senior Data Engineer for our Platform team. We are a B2B SaaS company and our data platform serves about sixty analysts, data scientists, and a growing set of customer-facing analytics features. You will own a major slice of our transformation layer and warehouse reliability. Two near-term problems: our warehouse compute cost has been growing faster than usage and leadership wants it controlled without degrading freshness, and our data-quality incidents (a bad number reaching a customer-facing dashboard) are too frequent and we lack the lineage to triage them quickly. We want an engineer who can talk specifically about how they made a warehouse cheaper or more reliable — what they profiled, what they changed, what the measurable result was, and what tradeoff they accepted. In the interview we will go deep on one project: the decisions, the mechanism, and how you verified the outcome. We expect you to reason about tradeoffs explicitly rather than describe a tidy success story.

history.q: Give me the shape of your current role at Meridian — team size, what you own, who consumes your data.
You list a warehouse cost-reduction effort. What triggered it and what was the state when you started?
How did you decide which queries or models to attack first?
Walk me through the single change that gave you the biggest cost reduction.
You moved heavy models from full-refresh to incremental. What's the failure mode of incremental models, and how did you guard against it?
Clustering the hot tables — how did you choose the clustering keys, and how did you confirm it actually helped?
What did all this cost you? Any correctness or freshness tradeoff you had to accept to get the savings?
You said spend 'came down meaningfully.' Put a number on it — percentage or dollars, and over what period.
If you had to redo incremental models on a table with frequent late-arriving and back-dated records, how would you design it to stay correct?
Suppose six months after your cost work, spend is creeping back up. Walk me through how you'd diagnose where the regression is coming from.

candidate_last_answer: Hm. Give me a second on that one. ... Okay. Honestly, before I answer how I'd diagnose the creep, can I turn it around and ask you something? When you say spend is creeping back up at Northwind — do you currently have query-level attribution, like can you tie warehouse cost back to a specific model, a specific consumer, or a tag? Because my whole diagnostic approach forks on that answer. If you already have per-query cost tagging and a usage history, the diagnosis is basically a top-movers analysis and I can describe that. But if you don't have that instrumentation — which, candidly, is the more common situation — then step one isn't diagnosis at all, it's building the attribution before you can even see the regression, and that's a different conversation. So which of those worlds are we in at Northwind today?

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: my whole diagnostic approach forks on that answer

expected-yield: redirect him to walk both branches concretely instead of stalling on the interviewer's answer — make him lay out the diagnostic steps when per-query attribution exists (top-movers, usage-vs-cost delta) and the steps when it doesn't (how he'd stand up cost tagging first), demonstrating the actual diagnostic mechanism rather than deferring it
